pattern: Multi-source BFS from all water cells assigns each land cell its distance to the nearest water, which maximizes the tallest peak.
hints: Water cells are fixed at zero and neighbours may differ by at most one, so a cell's height can be at most its distance to the nearest water.
To make the highest peak as tall as possible, push every cell up to that limit simultaneously.
Run a breadth-first search seeded from all water cells at once; each cell's BFS layer number is its height.

edge_inputs.description: small grid with one water cell
two water cells shaping the peaks
single row, water at one end
single column, water in the middle
water in the center radiating outward

stress.description: random grid with a guaranteed water cell
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 30))
    n = max(1, min(size, 30))
    grid = [[1 if rng.random() < 0.15 else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    if not any(1 in row for row in grid):
        grid[rng.randrange(m)][rng.randrange(n)] = 1
    return [grid]
